## Target's compacted history
The target's earlier work has been folded into the `<summary>` block(s) below — this is what the target itself sees at the head of its context, NOT verbatim transcript. Use it to understand the arc: what was decided, what constraints were set, what was already done. If the window below shows the target acting on an assumption that CONTRADICTS a summary, re-doing work a summary says was completed, or ignoring a constraint a summary records — that is context drift; flag it. Do NOT trace values inside the summaries (they're compacted, not tool-verifiable). Do NOT emit `msg_idx` pointing into this section — `msg_idx` is window-relative. If a summary's paraphrase is ambiguous on the point you're checking, `query_target_history(summary=<id>, question=…)` fetches the original behind it — use sparingly.

<summary id=6701gdok>
Undermind-MCP review & brief-authoring session — evaluating a user's private MCP server and producing hand-off briefs for Claude Code

## Narrative

**Opening (Undermind capability question).** The user asked whether the agent knew Undermind.app; agent described it as an AI literature-search agent (undermind.ai / app.undermind.ai). The user then asked <system>Is there any way I could enable you to use it? It's a highly useful tool.</system> `USER:` (verbatim human portion) "Is there any way I could enable you to use it? It's a highly useful tool." Agent explained it has no self-serve API/MCP for individuals (enterprise API only, GSK case study cited); the manual-bridge fallback; and that it could run literature sweeps directly (OpenAlex/Crossref/arXiv/Semantic Scholar). An Auditor finding claimed the FRB-manuscript detail was ungrounded; agent rebutted (correctly) that it comes from the Project Context (twelve CHIME/DSA-110 co-detected FRBs, scattering/scintillation/energetics AJ manuscript). No fix needed.

**Repo investigation begins.** <human>Look into this whether we can or should install this MCP server tool I started developing for Undermind: https://github.com/jakobtfaber/undermind-mcp</human> `USER:` "Look into this whether we can or should install this MCP server tool I started developing for Undermind: https://github.com/jakobtfaber/undermind-mcp". Git clone was blocked (coarse git-protection mode — too many repos under the granted paths). Agent fell back to the GitHub API using `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`gh` CLI not installed). Repo metadata: private Python repo, default branch `main`, pushed 2026-07-08, size 65 KB, no license reported at first call. Full file tree pulled (see Literals).

**Files read across several fetches.** README (session-reuse bridge: Chrome MV3 companion → loopback receiver → `api.undermind.ai`; "personal use only," "may violate Undermind's ToS," "Under construction — Phase 1"); `pyproject.toml`; `configs/INSTALL.md`; `configs/claude_desktop_config.json`; `.env.example`; then `server.py` + `client.py` (filed, read in full via read_file); then `auth_store.py` + `http_receiver.py` (filed, read in full); then `manifest.json`, `background.js`, `popup.js`, `cli.py`; then `project-outline.md` (19568 chars) and `tests/fixtures/endpoints.md` (7546 chars). The agent identified the architecture and several bugs. Key code facts that drove everything downstream:
- `server.py` module-level `auth_store = AuthStore(use_keychain=True)` — keychain persistence hardcoded ON, unconditional; `init --use-keychain` flag cosmetic (never threaded). Violates `project-outline.md` §5 "Do not persist credentials to the keychain by default."
- `_cmd_reset` builds `AuthStore()` (keychain OFF) so `clear()` skips `_delete_from_keychain()` — reset doesn't purge the keychain.
- `undermind_create_search` mixes V1/V2: creates a V2 project, then POSTs V1 `/search/submit/`, returns that V1 id as `job_id`; but `get_status`/`get_results` read V2 `/projects/{id}/jobs/{job_id}/` and `/papers/` — ids don't line up.
- `_cmd_doctor` builds a fresh keychain-OFF `AuthStore`, never loads → always "session missing."
- `update_from_set_cookie` only matches `csrftoken`, but `endpoints.md` says the CSRF cookie is `app-csrftoken` → rotation missed → stale `X-CSRFToken` → 403.
- Fixture dirs empty (only `.gitkeep`); models inferred from SPA bundle dated 2026-04-23, unvalidated. Bundle-hash drift check specified but unimplemented. `wait_for` capped 120s.
- Good-as-is: companion token (`secrets.token_urlsafe(32)`, chmod 600, `compare_digest`), receiver bound `127.0.0.1:8787`, CORS locked, secret redaction + tested redactor, untrusted-wrapping of returned text, conservative rate limits, refusal to automate login.

Agent's long assessment (<assistant>I've read the whole repo at the current `main` (private, Python, last pushed today). Here's my assessment across both your questions — **can** I use it, and **should** you.

## What you've actually built

A three-part local system, all on your own machine:

- **Chrome MV3 companion extension** (`undermind-companion/`) — reads your `undermind.ai` session cookies + `app-csrftoken`, filters out analytics cookies, and POSTs them to a loopback endpoint every 10 min and on cookie change.
- **Loopback HTTP receiver** (`http_receiver.py`) — a Starlette app bound to `127.0.0.1:8787` that receives those cookies behind a companion token and stashes them in an in-memory `AuthStore`.
- **stdio MCP server + client** (`server.py`, `client.py`) — replays your browser session against `api.undermind.ai/api/v1` and `/api/v2`, exposing `undermind_list_projects / get_project / create_search / get_status / wait_for / get_results / ask / export_bibtex`.

It's a **session-reuse bridge**: it impersonates your logged-in browser to reach Undermind's private (reverse-engineered) API, because there's no self-serve public API. The README states this plainly — "Under construction," personal-use-only, and "may violate Undermind's Terms of Service."

## Can I use it from here? No — and it's architectural, not a permissions toggle

This is designed for **Claude Code / Cursor on your laptop**, and several hard constraints prevent it from working inside this Claude Science environment:

1. **It needs a live Chrome + loaded extension + your logged-in session.** None of that exists in this sandbox. The credentials only flow if a real browser is pushing them to `127.0.0.1:8787`.
2. **My connectors are MCP servers attached via Customize → Connectors**, and the network here is restricted to an allowlist of scientific-data domains. `api.undermind.ai` and a local `:8787` receiver are both outside it, and there's no browser in-sandbox to supply the cookies. So even if the stdio server were registered, it couldn't reach Undermind or get a session.
3. The tool's own design goal is to drive *the agent running on your machine* — i.e., Claude Code — not this hosted runtime.

So the only realistic deployment is **you running it locally in Claude Code/Cursor**, where it augments *that* agent. It's not something I connect to from here.

## Should you run it locally? The risk ledger

The security engineering is genuinely careful — worth saying, because it's better than most session-reuse hacks:

- Companion token (`secrets.token_urlsafe(32)`, chmod 600, constant-time compare); receiver bound strictly to `127.0.0.1`; CORS locked to `app.undermind.ai` + `chrome-extension://`.
- Secrets redacted in logs and `__repr__`, with a tested redactor; in-memory creds by default, keychain opt-in; MCP tools never return raw auth material.
- Undermind's returned text is wrapped `{"source":"undermind","untrusted":true}` so a downstream agent won't execute instructions hidden in abstracts; snippets truncated to 200 chars (sensible copyright posture); conservative rate limits; explicitly refuses to automate login or defeat bot detection.

But four risks are **inherent to the approach** and can't be engineered away:

1. **Terms of Service.** Reverse-engineered private endpoints driven by a lifted browser session is the textbook definition of what SaaS ToS prohibit. The realistic downside is account suspension. The tool makes you type "I understand" precisely because this is real.
2. **Bot detection / Cloudflare.** The client watches for challenge pages and raises `BotChallenge` — good defensive behavior, but if Undermind tightens detection the tool breaks, and repeated triggering is exactly what flags an account.
3. **Bundle-hash drift.** `endpoints.md` says the API was reverse-engineered from the SPA bundle on **2026-04-23**. It's now July — those endpoints may already have shifted. The `doctor` bundle-hash warning exists because this *will* rot silently.
4. **Credential blast radius.** Even done well, you're pulling your session cookie out of the browser's protected store into a Python process (and optionally the OS keychain), widening the attack surface on your Undermind account.

## One concrete correctness bug worth fixing before you rely on it

The `create → status → results` pipeline mixes the two API versions and looks non-functional end-to-end:

- `undermind_create_search` creates a **V2** project, then calls `client.submit_search()` which POSTs to **V1** `/search/submit/` (the "classic search") — an endpoint not tied to the project it just created — and returns that V1 response's `id` as `job_id`.
- But `undermind_get_status` → `get_job()` reads **V2** `/projects/{id}/jobs/{job_id}/`, and `undermind_get_results` reads **V2** `/projects/{id}/papers/`. A V1 classic-search id won't resolve as a V2 job, and the classic results aren't attached to that V2 project.

Your `client.py` already has the correct V2 job primitives (`get_job`, `submit_job` on a job id) — the tool just doesn't wire them together. This is likely why it's still "Phase 1." Also note the `tests/fixtures/curl/` and `responses/` dirs are empty (only `.gitkeep`), so the pydantic models were inferred from the bundle and **never validated against live responses** — Step 6 ("STOP AND ASK: endpoint capture") of your own build plan hasn't been run. That's the highest-value next step: capture real request/response pairs and reconcile the models + the V1/V2 flow.

## My recommendation

- **For this environment:** it can't be plugged in. If you want Undermind-style recall while working with me, I'd run the literature sweep directly (OpenAlex, Crossref, arXiv, Semantic Scholar) and hand back a cited artifact bibliography.
- **For local Claude Code use:** it's plausibly usable *after* you (a) capture live endpoints and fix the V1/V2 create-flow bug, and (b) accept the ToS/ban risk knowingly. The safety scaffolding is already solid enough that the remaining risk is the approach itself, not the implementation.
- **The path I'd actually pursue first:** email Undermind for API access. Their enterprise API is real (the GSK deployment is a public case study). A sanctioned key eliminates the ToS exposure, the Cloudflare fragility, and the bundle-drift maintenance burden in one move — and if they issue one, *that* I can wire in here cleanly (key in Customize → Credentials, domain allowlisted, done). The browser-companion is the right fallback only if they decline individuals.

Want me to (a) draft the endpoint-capture checklist + a corrected `create_search` that uses the V2 job flow, or (b) draft the Undermind API-access request email?</assistant>) concluded: can't be wired into this hosted environment (needs live Chrome + logged-in session; `api.undermind.ai`/`:8787` off-allowlist); run-locally is plausible after fixing bugs and accepting ToS/ban risk; the durable path is a sanctioned Undermind API key.

**Auditor finding (keychain).** Agent's earlier "in-memory creds by default, keychain opt-in" claim was flagged as contradicting `AuthStore(use_keychain=True)`. Agent acknowledged, corrected the record (persistence ON by default), and added that `_cmd_reset` won't delete the keychain entry it wrote.

**Fix-list.** <human>Yes, write up the corrected fix list.</human> `USER:` "Yes, write up the corrected fix list." Agent wrote and saved `undermind-mcp-fixlist.md` (8 items, P0/P1/P2, each where→what→why→fix). Artifact saved (see Literals). Reply summarized all 8 items; noted items 3/4/5 were newly surfaced by re-reading, 1/2 were the keychain thread.

**Capture checklist + sanitizer hardening (major sub-thread).** <human>yes, draft endpoint-secure capture checklist.</human> `USER:` "yes, draft endpoint-secure capture checklist." Agent wrote `undermind-mcp-capture-checklist.md` (7 steps + pre-commit gate + embedded `sanitize.py`). It then **tested the sanitizer in the sandbox** rather than shipping untested:
- First run on a synthetic HAR: redaction worked, "Secret scan clean," reported CSRF cookie `app-csrftoken`.
- Negative test (disable redaction) initially inconclusive — a false alarm because the "break" edited a non-matching line and the secrets only lived in header/`Set-Cookie` positions never written to output files.
- A URL/body-token HAR exposed a **real blind spot**: `?token=LEAKtoken1234567890abcdefGHIJ` (29 chars) survived — matched no specific pattern and under the 32-char generic net; the gate reused the same patterns so couldn't catch it.
- Agent hardened patterns (added sensitive-key `key=value` and `"key":"value"` forms, tightened `ALLOW`) and fixed `redact_obj` to redact by sensitive-key match (not just header names). Re-ran a 4-case battery (A cookie/CSRF+rotation, B URL token, C nested JSON secrets, D redaction-disabled → gate fires RC 1): **all passed**. Confirmed embedded script == tested script (byte-identical). Saved the checklist artifact.

**Sequencing question.** <human>should I hand `undermind-mcp-capture-checklist.md` to  Claude Code now?</human> `USER:` "should I hand `undermind-mcp-capture-checklist.md` to Claude Code now?" Agent: not yet — Steps 1–4 are human (browser/HAR), only `sanitize.py`+hook and later model-reconciliation are Claude Code's; and don't run `serve`/`doctor` during capture before fixing the P0 keychain bug (it'd persist cookies). Recommended order: (1) P0 fixes via Claude Code, (2) manual capture, (3) Claude Code reconciles fixtures → fixes #3/#5.

**Automation ask.** <human>I want to convert all human tasks to automatable agent-driven tasks and remove the need for manual intervention on my part.</human> `USER:` "I want to convert all human tasks to automatable agent-driven tasks and remove the need for manual intervention on my part." Agent: most automatable; the DevTools capture can become a passive auto-capture browser extension; but **initial login is irreducibly human by design** — `project-outline.md` §5 forbids automating login/headless-login/synthetic-keepalive, it's the ban tripwire, and the agent independently won't build auth-automation/bot-detection-evasion. Offered (a) auto-capture extension spec, (b) P0-fix brief for Claude Code, (c) Undermind API-access email. <human>yes to (a) and (b) --- no to (c)</human> `USER:` "yes to (a) and (b) --- no to (c)".

**Brief authoring (<e85>, the large final assistant turn + <e86>).** Agent wrote two documents. It first authored the **auto-capture spec** `undermind-mcp-autocapture-spec.md` (fetch-wrap in MAIN world because MV3 `chrome.webRequest` can't read response bodies; ISOLATED relay; service-worker authoritative redactor; receiver `POST /capture` with server-side gate; `chrome.debugger` fallback; natural cookie-invisibility property). It then **installed Node.js** (via `manage_packages` install of `nodejs` into env `python` — this was a tool call, not a bash cell, which is why a later Auditor couldn't see it in the exec log) and ran the JS redactor through a 10-assertion battery in a real engine — all passed. Then wrote the **P0-fix brief** `undermind-mcp-p0-brief.md` (scoped to `server.py`+`cli.py`; marker-file `keychain_enabled` written at init, read at serve, default off; reset force-purges keychain+marker; 5 mocked tests; two named commits). Both artifacts saved (see Literals). <e86> is the edit_file creating the P0 brief.

## Note on events after this chunk
This span's listed events end at <e86> (P0 brief file created). Everything the user and Claude Code did afterward — handing off the P0 brief, Claude Code returning fixes on branch `fix/keychain-persistence-p0`, PR #1, the batch-2 brief, Part A on `fix/config-constants-and-create-flow`, the auto-capture brief with the UUID/author redactor fixes, and the final Auditor findings about `scan_for_secrets` — is outside this chunk and recorded elsewhere.

─── user messages (verbatim) ───
USER[e2]: Are you familiar with the web-based tool known as Undermind.app ?
USER[e4]: Is there any way I could enable you to use it? It's a highly useful tool.
USER[e10]: Look into this whether we can or should install this MCP server tool I started developing for Undermind: https://github.com/jakobtfaber/undermind-mcp
USER[e47]: Yes, write up the corrected fix list.
USER[e56]: yes, draft endpoint-secure capture checklist.
USER[e79]: should I hand `undermind-mcp-capture-checklist.md` to Claude Code now?
USER[e82]: I want to convert all human tasks to automatable agent-driven tasks and remove the need for manual intervention on my part.
USER[e84]: yes to (a) and (b) --- no to (c)

## Immediately preceding context

Claude Code delivered the auto-capture branch. I can't pull the diff yet — it's local-only — so this is an assessment of the deviations and the plan, then push-to-review as with the last two branches.

**All three deviations are fine — one is actually an improvement:**

1. **Redactor in `redactor.mjs`, imported by `background.js` (not inlined).** This is *better* than what I specced. My brief's "byte-for-byte equivalent" worry was about JS↔JS drift between two inlined copies; a single imported module removes the possibility of drift entirely, which is the stronger guarantee. The consequence — `background.js` becomes a module service worker (`"type": "module"` in the manifest) — is legitimate MV3 (module service workers are supported) and only matters operationally: you reload at `chrome://extensions` to pick it up. Since the authoritative redactor only runs in the service worker (the tap just drops `X-CSRFToken`, the relay only forwards), a module import there is exactly the right place for it. Approve.
2. **`test_sanitize.py` instead of `test_redactor.py`** — avoiding a collision with the existing logging-redactor tests is correct. Clobbering an unrelated test file to match my suggested name would've been worse. Approve.
3. **JS test as a standalone `node` script** — no JS test framework exists in the repo, and adding one to run one file is scope creep. `node undermind-companion/redactor.test.mjs` is fine as a documented invocation. Approve.

**The security posture landed as specified** — page-side early redaction + receiver-side authoritative gate, `test_gate_rejects_when_redaction_bypassed` proving the gate fires when redaction is defeated, fixtures to `~/.config/…/captures/` never the repo, no new permissions. That gate test is the one I most wanted to see after last round's bug, so good that it's there and green.

**Two things I'll confirm when I read the actual diff** (not blockers, just what I check):
- The manifest module-worker declaration and that `redactor.mjs` resolves correctly from the service worker (relative import, present in the extension dir).
- That the gate in the shipped `_sanitize.py` matches the value-only pattern set we corrected — i.e. `cite_key` survives and the substring bug didn't creep back in. Its own unit test should pin this, but I'll eyeball it.

**Answer: push it stacked** (no PR, no merge), same as `fix/config-constants-and-create-flow`. I'll pull the branch diff and give it the same code-level review I gave the other two before anything merges. The whole stack — P0, config-constants, auto-capture — merges to `main` together after the combined review.

One loose end to confirm: the **fourth-branch close-failure test** (the `async def` one exercising `_on_close_done` via `task.exception()`) was a follow-up commit for the *config-constants* branch — did that land, or did it get folded into this work? If it's not in yet, tack it onto the config-constants branch before we do the combined review, so that branch's coverage is complete.

Push when ready and I'll review the diff.
